lessons.name: The 4-step interview frame
URL shortener — designed in 10 minutes
Rate limiter — token bucket, sliding window, GCRA, and edge enforcement
News feed — push vs pull vs hybrid
Caching pitfalls — the six that come up in interviews
Chat / messaging at scale
Consistent hashing — how to shard without re-shuffling
CAP & PACELC — what you can't have all of
Back-of-envelope — the numbers every senior knows cold
DB internals — B-tree vs LSM, when each wins
Replication & Raft — consensus in one page
Reliability patterns — circuit breaker, bulkhead, retry budget
Distributed log — Kafka partitioning, replication, exactly-once
Geo-dispatch — Uber-style matching at scale
Payment processing — idempotency, sagas, the exactly-once myth
Notification system — how the pieces fit
Cell-based architecture — blast radius as a first-class design constraint
Outbox pattern + CDC — transactional event publishing
Tail-latency engineering — hedged requests + p99 thinking
Distributed tracing — OpenTelemetry + W3C trace context
Multi-tenancy — what it means and why it matters
Webhook reliability — making someone else's server your problem
SSO for enterprise — what SAML, OIDC, SCIM actually do
Customer VPC deploys — four shapes, escalating cost (incl. BYOC)
Event sourcing + CQRS — when the log IS the database
gRPC vs REST vs GraphQL — pick the wire format
Customer's legacy ERP has no API — the integration ladder
Customer-facing eval & monitoring — the AI observability stack

cards.q: What is the 4-step system design interview frame?
cards.a: (1) Requirements: functional + non-functional (QPS, latency, consistency). (2) Capacity estimate: back-of-envelope numbers. (3) High-level boxes: API, services, storage. (4) Deep-dive on one hard subproblem the interviewer cares about.

cards.q: Multi-tenancy: silo vs pool vs bridge?
cards.a: Silo: dedicated DB per tenant (max isolation, highest cost). Pool: shared DB + shared schema with a tenant_id column (cheapest, noisy-neighbor risk). Bridge: shared DB instance with separate schema per tenant (logical isolation, easier per-tenant migrations). Match the pattern to the customer's compliance posture.

cards.q: Webhook reliability checklist?
cards.a: HMAC-sign each payload, require idempotency keys, exponential backoff retries with a DLQ, dashboard for replay, signed timestamps with a skew window (e.g., 5 min) to block replay, and a documented delivery-latency SLA.

cards.q: Consistent hashing: cost of adding the Nth node?
cards.a: Roughly 1/N of keys remap to the new node. With virtual nodes (typically 100-200 vnodes per physical server), the load redistributes evenly across all existing nodes instead of being pulled from a single neighbor.

cards.q: CAP vs PACELC in one sentence each?
cards.a: CAP: during a partition, choose Consistency or Availability. PACELC adds: Else (no partition), choose Latency or Consistency. PACELC is more useful because it covers steady-state tradeoffs, not just failure mode.

cards.q: Feed fan-out: push vs pull vs hybrid?
cards.a: Push (fan-out-on-write): low read latency, write amplification for celebrities. Pull (fan-out-on-read): cheap writes, expensive reads for users who follow many. Hybrid: push for normal users, pull for celebrities above a follower threshold.

cards.q: Why are idempotency keys non-negotiable for side-effecting APIs?
cards.a: Every charge_card / send_email / deploy call carries a client-generated unique key. The server stores (key, response) in a dedupe table; duplicate requests return the cached response. Without this, network retries and agent loops cause double-charges.

cards.q: Failure mode of per-server in-memory rate limiting?
cards.a: Each server enforces independently, so a client hitting N servers can do N times the limit. A customer can blow past a 100 req/sec cap by spreading 1k req/sec across 10 instances. Fix: centralized Redis sliding-window with a Lua script for atomicity.

cards.q: Which multi-tenancy pattern fits a regulated enterprise customer demanding data residency?
cards.a: Silo (DB-per-tenant) or at minimum bridge (schema-per-tenant). Pool fails because tenant_id filtering is enforced in app code, which auditors treat as insufficient isolation for HIPAA, SOC 2 Type II, or GDPR data-residency claims.

cards.q: What celebrity-threshold heuristic is used for hybrid feed fan-out?
cards.a: Above some follower count (e.g., 1M+), switch that user from push to pull. Their followers query the celeb's timeline at read time and merge with their pushed feed, capping write amplification per post.

cards.q: When does the CAP tradeoff actually bind?
cards.a: Only during a network partition. On a healthy network you can have strong consistency AND availability. The CAP choice is what your system does when nodes can't talk: serve stale/divergent data (AP) or refuse writes (CP).

cards.q: PACELC classification for Cassandra vs Spanner?
cards.a: Cassandra: PA/EL (available during partitions, low-latency in steady state; tunable per-query). Spanner: PC/EC (consistent during partitions, consistent in steady state via TrueTime + Paxos). Different defaults, different use cases.

cards.q: Why virtual nodes in consistent hashing?
cards.a: Without vnodes, adding a single node pulls ~1/N of the load from one neighbor, creating hot spots. With ~100-200 vnodes per physical node, the new node draws load evenly from every existing node, and removal is the inverse.

cards.q: Why is true exactly-once delivery impossible across systems?
cards.a: Network failures make it impossible to distinguish 'message delivered, ack lost' from 'message lost.' Settle for at-least-once + idempotent receivers (the standard answer), or at-most-once if loss is acceptable. Kafka's 'exactly-once' is scoped to a single Kafka transaction.

cards.q: Two main cache stampede mitigations?
cards.a: (1) Singleflight / request coalescing: only one request rebuilds the value; others wait on its result. (2) Refresh-ahead: rebuild before TTL expires, serving the stale value during recomputation. Both prevent thundering herds against the origin.

cards.q: How do you fix a hot key in Redis?
cards.a: Replicate the hot key across N shards (key_1..key_N) and have clients pick a random replica for reads. Consistent hashing doesn't help when traffic concentrates on ONE key, since by definition that key maps to one node.

cards.q: Why Redis + Lua for cross-server rate limiting?
cards.a: Lua scripts run atomically on the Redis server, so the read-modify-write of a sliding-window counter happens without race conditions across N application servers. In-memory per-server limits can't enforce a global cap.

cards.q: Token bucket vs sliding-window counter for rate limits?
cards.a: Token bucket: allows bursts up to bucket size, then refills at a steady rate (good for API quotas that should absorb spikes). Sliding-window counter: cheap and approximate, smooths traffic but rejects bursts. Pick by how much burstiness you want to tolerate.

cards.q: How to size the idempotency-key TTL on a webhook receiver?
cards.a: TTL must exceed the sender's max retry window. Stripe retries up to ~3 days, some platforms up to a week. Sized too short, late retries slip past the dedupe table and cause duplicate processing; sized too long, your storage grows.

cards.q: How do you order messages in a chat when two users send simultaneously?
cards.a: Server-assigned monotonic per-conversation sequence number, issued by whichever shard owns that conversation. Wall-clock timestamps drift between clients and servers; a deterministic sequence guarantees the same ordering for everyone.

cards.q: Range sharding vs hash sharding: when range?
cards.a: Range sharding when queries need ordered scans (BETWEEN, ORDER BY date). Risk: hot partitions when writes concentrate on a recent range (today's date). Hash sharding distributes evenly but destroys range queries and cross-shard scans.

cards.q: Read-replica lag breaks read-your-writes; standard fix?
cards.a: Session pinning: route a user's reads to the primary for N seconds after their write (or until a known LSN reaches the replica). Other users keep reading from replicas, so you preserve read scale while making the writer see their own update.

cards.q: Saga pattern for distributed transactions?
cards.a: Break a transaction into a sequence of local steps, each with a compensating action. On failure, run compensations in reverse order to undo prior steps. Standard when 2PC is impractical (cross-service, cross-region, third-party APIs).

cards.q: Goal and three states of a circuit breaker?
cards.a: Goal: prevent cascading failures when a downstream is unhealthy. States: CLOSED (calls pass through), OPEN (after N failures, fast-fail without calling downstream), HALF_OPEN (probe with limited traffic; on success close, on failure re-open).

cards.q: Event sourcing: where is the source of truth?
cards.a: An append-only event log is the source of truth. Current state is derived by replaying events. Snapshots and read-model projections (CQRS) are derived views built for fast reads, and can be rebuilt from the log at any time.

cards.q: What back-of-envelope latency numbers should you know cold?
cards.a: L1 cache ~1 ns, main memory ~100 ns, SSD random read ~100 us, network round-trip within a DC ~0.5 ms, cross-region RTT ~50-150 ms, disk seek ~10 ms. These determine whether a design needs caching, replication, or async.

cards.q: Quick QPS estimate for 100M DAU with 10 requests per user per day?
cards.a: 100M * 10 = 1B requests/day. 1B / 86400 ~= 11.5k QPS average. Peak is typically 2-5x average, so plan for ~30-60k QPS. Capacity-plan to peak, not average.

cards.q: Token bucket vs leaky bucket: difference?
cards.a: Token bucket: tokens accumulate up to a max; bursts allowed up to bucket size. Leaky bucket: requests drain at a fixed rate from a queue; bursts are smoothed but queued requests add latency. Token bucket is more common for API quotas; leaky bucket for traffic shaping.

cards.q: Cache-aside vs write-through vs write-behind?
cards.a: Cache-aside: app reads cache; on miss reads DB + populates cache (most common). Write-through: writes go to cache + DB synchronously (consistent, slower writes). Write-behind: writes hit cache; DB updated async (fast, risks loss on crash).

cards.q: LRU vs LFU eviction: when each wins?
cards.a: LRU: evicts least-recently-used; good for recency-biased workloads (news, sessions). LFU: evicts least-frequently-used; good for stable hot sets where one-time bursts shouldn't evict popular items. LFU needs decay (e.g., TinyLFU) to forget old frequencies.

cards.q: Sync vs async replication tradeoff?
cards.a: Sync: primary waits for replica ack before responding (zero data loss, higher latency, write stalls if replica is slow). Async: primary responds immediately, replicates in background (low latency, RPO > 0 on primary failure). Semi-sync waits for one replica.

cards.q: Raft leader election: when does a new election start?
cards.a: Each follower has a randomized election timeout (e.g., 150-300 ms). If no heartbeat arrives from the leader, the follower increments its term, becomes a candidate, and requests votes. Randomization prevents split votes; majority quorum is required to win.

cards.q: Raft vs Paxos: practical difference?
cards.a: Both achieve consensus over an asynchronous network with majority quorum. Raft is designed for understandability: explicit leader, log replication, and leader election as named subproblems. Paxos (Multi-Paxos) is older, more flexible, but notoriously hard to implement correctly.

cards.q: 2PC vs saga for distributed transactions?
cards.a: 2PC (two-phase commit): prepare + commit across all participants; blocks if coordinator fails, requires XA-capable resources. Saga: chain of local transactions with compensations; non-blocking, eventually consistent, no global lock. Sagas dominate microservice architectures.

cards.q: What problem does the transactional outbox pattern solve?
cards.a: Dual-write inconsistency: if you write to DB then publish to Kafka, a crash between the two leaves them out of sync. Outbox writes the event to an 'outbox' table in the same DB transaction; a relay (or CDC) reads the table and publishes, guaranteeing at-least-once with the DB commit.

cards.q: CDC (change data capture) vs polling for outbox?
cards.a: Polling reads the outbox table on a schedule (simple, adds latency, can miss with bad cursors). CDC (Debezium, Postgres logical replication) tails the WAL and streams changes in near real-time without app changes. CDC is the modern default for high-volume systems.

cards.q: Kafka partitioning: how is ordering guaranteed?
cards.a: Ordering is guaranteed only within a single partition. Producers pick partition by key hash (same key always goes to same partition). To preserve per-entity order (e.g., per user), use that entity's ID as the partition key. Cross-partition order is not guaranteed.

cards.q: Kafka vs SQS vs Kinesis: pick which when?
cards.a: Kafka: high throughput, replayable log, consumer groups, you operate it (or MSK/Confluent). SQS: simple managed queue, no ordering (standard) or FIFO with low throughput. Kinesis: AWS-managed log similar to Kafka, integrated with AWS, lower ceiling. RabbitMQ: rich routing, lower throughput.

cards.q: Webhook signature verification: what goes into the signature?
cards.a: HMAC-SHA256 over (timestamp + raw body) with a shared secret. Receiver verifies: signature matches, timestamp within a skew window (e.g., 5 min) to block replay, and the secret is rotated periodically. Sign the raw body, not parsed JSON, so re-serialization doesn't break verification.

cards.q: SAML SP-init vs IDP-init flow?
cards.a: SP-initiated: user visits the app (SP), gets redirected to the IDP to authenticate, then back to the SP with a signed assertion. IDP-initiated: user starts at the IDP dashboard and clicks the app tile, IDP posts the assertion to the SP's ACS URL. SP-init is more secure (the SP issues the AuthnRequest).

cards.q: OIDC vs SAML: when to use OIDC?
cards.a: OIDC is OAuth 2.0 + identity layer (JWT ID tokens, JSON over HTTPS). SAML uses XML assertions, older, dominant in enterprise B2B. Use OIDC for modern web/mobile apps; offer SAML when selling to enterprises whose IDPs (Okta, Azure AD) are SAML-first. SCIM handles user provisioning for both.

cards.q: BYOC (bring-your-own-cloud) deploy: what does the vendor manage?
cards.a: Vendor's control plane (in vendor's account) orchestrates a data plane that runs inside the customer's VPC/account. Customer data never leaves their cloud. Vendor uses cross-account IAM roles for management. Costs the vendor more (per-customer infra) but unlocks regulated industries.

cards.q: URL shortener: short-code generation strategies?
cards.a: (1) Hash(URL) + base62, truncated to N chars, collision check (simple, deterministic). (2) Auto-increment ID + base62 (compact, leaks volume). (3) Pre-generate pool of random codes, hand out from queue (no contention). Use 7 base62 chars for ~3.5T codes.

cards.q: Hedged requests for p99 reduction?
cards.a: Send the request to one replica; if no response within (e.g.) p95 latency, send a duplicate to a second replica and take whichever returns first. Cuts p99 dramatically with low extra load. Originated in Google's 'The Tail at Scale.' Cancel the loser to save work.

cards.q: W3C trace context: what header propagates the trace?
cards.a: The 'traceparent' header carries version, trace-id (16 bytes), parent-span-id (8 bytes), and flags. Every service must propagate it on outgoing calls so spans across services join into one trace. 'tracestate' carries vendor-specific data. OpenTelemetry SDKs handle this automatically.

cards.q: Three pillars of observability and what each answers?
cards.a: Logs: what happened (discrete events, high cardinality, expensive at scale). Metrics: how much / how often (time series, aggregated, cheap, good for alerting). Traces: how a request flowed across services (latency breakdown, dependency mapping). You need all three; none subsumes the others.

cards.q: Cell-based architecture: what is a 'cell'?
cards.a: An isolated, full-stack slice of the system (compute + DB + cache) that serves a partition of users. Cells share nothing at runtime; a router maps tenant -> cell. Blast radius of a bad deploy or noisy tenant is capped to one cell. AWS, Slack, and Stripe use variants.

cards.q: gRPC vs REST vs GraphQL: pick one when?
cards.a: gRPC: internal service-to-service, strong typing, HTTP/2 streaming, low latency. REST: public APIs, cacheable, broadest client support. GraphQL: client-driven queries, avoids over/under-fetching, good for mobile and aggregation layers. Pick by client (browser/mobile/internal) and evolution needs.

cards.q: Vector search ANN indexes: HNSW vs IVF vs PQ?
cards.a: HNSW: hierarchical graph, high recall + low latency, high memory. IVF: cluster vectors into Voronoi cells, scan top-N cells (lower memory, tunable recall). PQ (product quantization): compress vectors into codes (8-16x smaller, lossy). Production stacks often combine IVF + PQ for billion-scale.

cards.q: Geo-indexing for ride-matching: which structure?
cards.a: Geohash or S2 cell IDs as the shard/index key. Drivers publish (cell_id, lat, lng) every few seconds to Redis sorted sets keyed by cell. To match a rider, query nearby cells (neighboring geohashes / S2 cover) and pick the closest driver. Quadtrees and R-trees are alternatives for variable-density regions.
